comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]It's getting on for midnight, so here we go with a new Magic Monday. The picture is of a portrait statue of En no Gyōja, the traditional founder of the Japanese occult tradition of Shugendo. Born in 634, he devoted his life to herbalism, ascetic practices in the high mountains, and a free mix of Shinto, Buddhist, and Taoist magical disciplines, earning a reputation as one of Japan's greatest occultists. Shugenja, as practitioners of Shugendo are known, still revere his memory and practice various forms of the occult tradition he founded. He's still sufficiently famous today to appear in anime from time to time; think of him as Japan's Merlin and you won't be too far off (though Kobo Daishi, who will doubtless be featured in an upcoming Magic Monday, also has a good claim to that title). 

Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer, though I'm going to have somewhat limited email access tomorrow and it may be Tuesday or even Wednesday before I get to them all. If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here.

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use the button below to access my online tip jar. 

With that said, have at it!   

****This Magic Monday is now closed -- and yes, that means you. See you next week!***

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Okay, first of all, this is one of the standard experiences of the early phases of the path. I don't think I've met a serious practitioner who skipped it. It's a temporary thing, and if you don't let yourself become too spooked by it, you'll be fine.

So the first thing to do is to remember that this is normal. Try to relax, and keep in mind that you don't have to fix everything at once.

The second thing is to look over the things you feel you need to change, and choose just one of them to work on for now. One of the basic rules of magic is that the will is strong when it's single. Yes, there are lots of things you want to get to and lots of changes you want to make, but you'll succeed best if you tackle them one at a time, patiently, and change things gradually and with an approach based on kindness toward yourself as well as toward others.

The third thing is to keep in mind that awareness really is the universal solvent of the alchemists. Now that you've begun to wake up, the simple fact that you see things you didn't previously see will have potent effects on how you relate to the world all by itself. Very often, we don't solve our problems -- we simply outgrow them -- and the work you're doing now aims, among other things, at helping you do that.

In doing so I have been tracing the pentagrams and circle in the air at the height where my arm naturally sits. I was curious as to whether this is the correct practice or whether I would benefit from actually tracing it out on the floor.

replies.title: Re: CGD LRP Practice
replies.content:
Tracing the pentagram in the air, so that it stands vertically before you, is the correct practice. You don't trace the pentagrams on the floor in the pentagram ritual!

As for visualization, the only way I know of is to practice it. Set aside ten minutes a day to imagine something: an object, a scene, a person, or what have you. Bring in all your imaginary senses, not just sight: hear, smell, feel, and (where appropriate) taste the thing you're imagining. Make the imagination as vivid as you can. Keep that up daily, and you'll pick up the skills you need.

comments.title: Crowns
comments.content: Just saw a photo of a crown looking like Queen Elizabeth’s in a theatre. Do you think the crowns and other insignia of ancient European kingdoms were magically consecrated?

replies.title: Re: Crowns
replies.content: In ancient times, of course they were. Crowns were originally magical objects of immense power, meant to focus certain carefully selected energies on the brain centers of those who wore them -- thus the role of gems, which focus planetary energies. It took many centuries and a lot of ignorance to reduce crowns to their present status of gaudy collectibles.

replies.title: Re: Crowns
replies.content: Fascinating!

replies.title: Re: Crowns
replies.content:
Kings and queens didn't simply become sovereigns upon the deaths of their predecessors back in the Middle Ages; first they had to undergo a complex ritual of coronation, which made use of one or several ancient crowns (and swords, too, and other mighty artifacts).

I still remember watching the coronation ion Queen Elizabeth II on our small B&W TV when I was a boy: even as a boy and even at the great distance of TV transmission, I was surprised and bowled over by the power of the ritual. I think it was the first time I had ever seen ceremonial magic in action. And thence got in the mail the special issue of Life Magazine with photographs and explanations of all the artifacts used in that ritual, which I devoured.

I don't happen to know of any good scholarly examination of all the many rituals of coronation from various parts of the Medieval world, but many of the rituals themselves have been preserved in manuscripts and early printed editions, and sometimes have even been edited by scholars. They would repay close study by someone knowledgeable about magic and how it works.

The crowns and swords themselves have often been preserved as state treasures (as in the UK) or as museum artifacts (as in France, IIRC).

And here and there, there are also ancient rituals involving coronations for lesser folk, not just for sovereigns-to-be. In the marriage ritual of the Russian Orthodox Church, the bride and groom are crowned with marriage crowns, and getting married is popularly called "crowning" (venchaniye).

replies.title: Re: Crowns
replies.content:
That's a very good point -- in most cases, iirc, the monarch became the political head of state as soon as the old monarch died, but took on the full inner side of the office only after the ritual of coronation was performed.

It would be particularly interesting to cross-reference European coronation rituals with the well-documented accession rituals of China, Japan, Thailand, and other east Asian monarchies, and also with the Egyptian rituals of enthronement, which iirc are also well documented.

comments.title: Time direction and reincarnation
comments.content:
Let's say that someone finishes one life and goes to the spiritual plane for a while before beginning the next. If they are beyond time and space after one life, does anything prevent them from entering the next life earlier in the perceived time line?

Or put another way, does anyone know of cases in which someone reincarnates and remembers details from a past life that are in the future?

replies.title: Re: Time direction and reincarnation
replies.content: I don't have that, but I do have a case of one life which died about 30 years after the next one was born. I have no idea how common it is, but apparently it can happen...

replies.title: Re: Time direction and reincarnation
replies.content: In theory, this can happen. In practice, I've yet to meet anyone who claims to remember a life in the future who isn't, consciously or subconsciously, engaging in some kind of wish-fulfillment fantasy.

replies.title: Re: Time direction and reincarnation
replies.content: How often do you see cases where past lives went "out of order" or overlapped? It may be that where these things happen the higher self simply won't allow access to such memories in any life which has "events in the future" from that life's perspective.

replies.title: Re: Time direction and reincarnation
replies.content: It's very, very rare. In every case I've personally looked into, lives are lived sequentially, with a gap of at least a few months and more usually some years between one death and another birth.

replies.title: Re: Time direction and reincarnation
replies.content:
Yes and yes. It's unusual, but it does happen for reasons that make sense to the Essence doing it.

In the past we had a body shortage - more Essences wanted lifetimes than there were bodies, and the results of that situation got written into the older tomes of occult lore as if that is the way it works.

Today, with a surfeit of bodies, it's not uncommon for one High Self to have several lifetimes running simultaneously - carefully isolated so that the don't contact each other.

John Roth

replies.title: (no subject)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Okay, there are two forms of affliction. A planet is essentially afflicted when it's in the sign of its detriment or fall -- for example, Mars in Libra or Taurus is in its detriment, and Mars in Cancer is in its fall. A planet is accidentally afflicted when it's retrograde, in a negative aspect (square or opposition) to another planet, or combust. A peregrine planet is in a position that gives it neither dignity nor debility; a peregrine planet can be seen as under a minor affliction.

If a planet is essentially afflicted in your natal chart, don't use an astrological talisman for that planet. If it's accidentally afflicted, pay attention to whether it also has accidental dignity -- say, a trine from another planet, or an angular location in the natal chart (i.e., in the 1st, 4th, 7th, or 10th houses) -- and weigh the accidental dignities and debilities before deciding whether to try a talisman or not. Natural magic isn't as sensitive to this, and neither is Golden Dawn-style ceremonial magic; this is specifically a rule for astrological magic of the sort Warnock teaches and practices.

comments.title: Address - and spiritual SciFi
comments.content:
Dear John Michael Greer,

I've got two questions to start with:

1. I don't like to address you with JMG, as most people here do - unless you do! What do you like best - JMG, John, John Michael, John Michael Greer, ...? Do you even care? ;-)

2. Until early adulthood I was a SciFi guy. Than I turned towards more holistic, spiritual thinking and today it's hard for me to read or watch SciFi at all, as most of it is very dominated by materialistic thinking. Rarely I find paranormal features - and practically nowhere I find a worldview, that mixes classic SciFi with Spirituality, Magic, Occultism. On the other hand I don't find spiritual / magical writers dealing with SciFi (outside of the Shadowrun Cyberpunk Universe and related fiction, that is, which mostly lacks depth, or so I remember from when I read the books many years ago).
And I see the same divergence in your publications: Your texts and interviews focus either on social / cultural / technological / environmental developments (including SciFi) - or on occult / magical / spiritual aspects. They hardly get intertwined. I loved your peace on "10 bio years from now" - but I was somewhat disappointed that you didn't break out of the classic scientific / materialist SciFi conventions (although I think I understand why you didn't).
So, my questions:
a) Why doesn't intertwine the material with the spiritual sphere more? Why don't you?
b) Any recommendations where I can find the kind of literature (fiction and non-fiction) that I'm looking for, so something that can be called "Spiritual SciFi"?

replies.title: Re: Address - and spiritual SciFi
replies.content:
Read Clark Ashton Smith, especially his Zothique stories. Xeethra is a good one to start with.

Actually Lovecraft is a good choice also. Maybe he didn't believe in his beasties, but I'm pretty sure they believed in him!

replies.title: Re: Address - and spiritual SciFi
replies.content:
1) I generally go by JMG online, so by all means use that.

2a) With very few exceptions -- my book The Blood of the Earth is one of them -- I've deliberately kept my magical writing separate from my social criticism. That's because I want the social criticism to influence people who will be put off by explicit talk about magic. Now that I've said most of what I want to say in the social-criticism sphere, that may be less important -- which is why I've blended occult and social/environmental perspectives in my current blog.

2b) Science fiction before 1980 or so used to be full of spiritual perspectives. They got thrown out with the trash once the atheist-materialist movement seized control of the genre and made it dull. Paranormal powers, alternate dimensions, the frontiers of human spiritual evolution were all grist for the mill. As a starting point, have you read CS Lewis' planetary trilogy? More generally, head for a good used book store that has a lot of old paperbacks and see what you can find; you may be amazed.

comments.title: Paucity of the Sea Themes in Druidry
comments.content:
Thanks as always for hosting these.

I've noticed that the nature spirituality of Druidry seems very land-based and there doesn't seem to be much discussion of the ocean. Sure, the sea shows up in "Land, Sky, Sea." It crops up in Celtic myth as well. But water, when it's mentioned, tends to be more focused on rivers, lakes, and springs. Even the salmon of wisdom seems to spend most of its time in a sacred pool. ;-)

Nwyfre exercises seem more oriented toward trees and mountains and springs, but again, I've never come across much on the sea. This is even more surprising given revival druidry comes from an island nation.

Do you know why there seems to be a gap in druidry when it comes to the ocean? Or perhaps there is no gap and I've just missed it?

replies.title: Re: Paucity of the Sea Themes in Druidry
replies.content: Sea energies are very challenging to work with in magic, and so most British magical traditions tend to keep instructional materials for that end of magic private, handing them out only to advanced students. You might find Dion Fortune's novel The Sea Priestess and her short story "The Sea Lure" relevant in this context.

replies.title: Re: Paucity of the Sea Themes in Druidry
replies.content: That’s very interesting to know. Thanks and I’ll have a look at those Dion Fortune works.

replies.title: Re: Paucity of the Sea Themes in Druidry
replies.content:
Speaking as a druid who has done a fair amount of sea magic: some druids do various forms of magic with sea energies, but most of us who do are pretty quiet about it. We don't talk about it in public much, and don't teach it to just anybody. The sea is immense and powerful, like the earth, but it's also changeable and moody and dangerous. (See: the folklore of premodern coastal and seagoing peoples for more details about the risks.) You have to be extremely careful in doing sea magic, and you have to know what you're doing. That's much less true of landlocked waters, although even there in some cases you face risks. (Again, see folklore for some of the dangers; in particular, the lore of the ceffyl-dwr and the kelpie.)

Also, of course, humans don't live in or on the sea. Magic using landlocked waters is more suited to our lives and living conditions because we're creatures of the land and the waters it holds.

replies.title: Re: Paucity of the Sea Themes in Druidry
replies.content: Thanks for this.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Okay, first of all, I hope you realize that you're in really serious trouble. This sort of thing is quite real, and unless you do some things you're probably not going to want to do, the rest of your life is going to be an accelerating spiral descending into misery and horror. I mean that quite literally. As things stand, you have a very high chance of ending up insane or in a suicide's grave.

Can you escape that? Sure. The thing you have to realize first is that by doing what you did, you've committed yourself to the Judeo-Christian dimension of the spiritual world -- the demons are part of that, after all. In order to get out of the trap you're in, you need to go to the other side. That means, if you come from a family that's historically Jewish, you need to head on down to a Conservative or Orthodox synagogue -- don't bother with Reform, they have no idea what to do for someone in your situation. Otherwise, you need to head on down to a Christian church, and here again, don't bother with the kind of liberal Christianity that doesn't believe in demons and isn't too sure about God. Talk to the rabbi or pastor about your situation, ask for help, and be willing to get down on your knees and accept the help of the god they worship. Then follow through on it -- as in, attend services, help support the church or synagogue, put time into daily prayer and study of the scriptures. Do that and you'll be fine.

Of course you'll lose all your atheist friends; that's going to happen anyway. You'll also lose all the wealth and power you got from the demon, present and prospective. You're going to lose those anyway -- it's standard practice for demons to wait until you're completely dependent on what they've given you, and then yank it out from under you at the moment when it will do you the most damage. They'll also put you in one position after another where you have to sacrifice everything that means anything to you to hang onto the wealth and power -- yes, and then snatch it away. That's what demons do. They're weak on our plane, which is why they have to work in the indirect ways they do, but what motivates them is malice, rage, and hatred. The sooner you get rid of their temporary gifts, the better.

I know this is probably not what you want to hear, but them's the breaks. As you've learned, when you do something like this, it doesn't matter whether or not you believe in demons; they believe in you...

comments.title: Aerial Spraying

replies.title: Re: Safety Razor
replies.content:
Welcome to traditional shaving! Since it's a clear retrovation, I hope our host will tolerate the diversion from magic.

For the razor itself, different razors definitely behave differently, but for me it doesn't matter too much at the end of the day. I end up using the first razor I ever got, an extremely cheap (as in under $10) Bailai I ordered from Maggard Razors, most often, although I've got more expensive razors since then. Whatever you get should be fine, as long as the blade stays in securely.

The blades, on the other hand, make a huge difference. In theory, everyone has different preferences and you should try a number of brands to find what works best with your razor and your face. In practice, you'll hear the word "Feather" so often you'll wonder how any other brand stays in business.

So yeah, Feather is the best. I'd still recommend trying a few other brands, though, and most places sell sample packs. In my case, I actually preferred slightly duller blades at first--Big Ben or Astra--but eventually moved to Feathers. Now shaving with anything but a Feather feels like trying to shave with a misery whip.

For shaving soap/cream, I recommend Castle Forbes Lavender. In addition to having the nicest lather, I find the lavender helps prevent the formation of little red bumps.

I'm amused to note that the other commentator's recommendations are quite similar.

I'd also recommend picking up a pre-shave oil and an aftershave. In my case, I use regular olive oil as a pre-shave and witch hazel as an aftershave.

Finally, I too felt like a "thank you" was appropriate at those points in the SoP and having been doing it that way.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Can you briefly discuss the concept of etheric vs. astral, or otherwise recommend a useful starting point/resources for the concepts of the various planes.

Thank you.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Sure. The etheric level is the subtle side of matter and life -- most traditional occult philosophy assigns physical matter and etheric substance to one plane, the physical plane, with solids, liquids, and gases as the three lower sub-levels of that plane and the four kinds of ether the four higher sub-levels. Etheric substance is "stuff" -- you can learn to feel it on your skin the way you'd feel air movements, and you can shape it the way you'd shape denser physical substances.

The astral realm is the realm we experience in imagination and in dreams. It's far subtler and more evasive than any of the substances of the physical/etheric realm. It's still dense enough to take on forms, which is why you can imagine a bright blue rabbit or dream about a giant pretzel that talks, but you have to sense it with your inner senses, not with the outer ones. Does that help?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Very helpful. I had just seen these terms (and used these terms) somewhat interchangeably, although I now realize the paths I follow tend toward the astral end of the spectrum.

Much obliged, as always, for your conducting these every week.

comments.title: Baptism and Karma Continued
comments.content:
Over the last few weeks there has been some discussion on the spiritual and karmic influences of baptism in which you said that baptism at an age where independent decision making comes into play sets up a permanent connection with the Christian God that affects the whole of that incarnation and has implications for afterlife experiences, and also that once that connection has been made, it places an obligation on the Christian that can have karmic implications over multiple lifetimes as well. One common practice in a lot of Protestant churches is to forego baptism until the “age of accountability,” which typically means sometime around the first onset of puberty.

This means that pretty much everyone who grew up in a mainline or fundamentalist branch of Protestant Christianity got baptized as a young adult (and quite possibly multiple times given the amount of guilt and fear that gets spread around in some of these churches, particularly with regards to the sorts of urges and emotions teenagers go through), and quite possibly didn’t seek out something else until sometime after they left for college. This seems to include a vast majority of neopagans I know and a lot of people I call friends and represented me up until I started studying Christian Hermeticism of various sorts a few years ago and finding that engaging with Christian scriptures, philosophies, and spiritual powers could lead to something other than fear, shame, and despair. This all leads to a few questions I still haven’t quite resolved yet though...

1: The idea that baptism as an adult essentially acts as a life-long contract with the Christian Cod that you’re accountable for stirs up some lurid imagery from the Christianity of my youth that probably isn’t what you had in mind when you said that. How would those afterlife / next life effects play out for formerly serious Christians who left Christianity as an adult with a bad taste in their mouth but cultivated a life-long non-Christian spiritual identity in some other religion and stuck with it? Just how dire a thing is apostasy from a karmic perspective?

2: I grew up in the types of holiness/Pentecostal churches where salvation was likened to blood on the doorposts of the Israelites protecting against a demiurgic Angel of Death of a God and every impure thought ranging from adolescent sexual urges to getting bored at church was enough to get you running scared to the pulpit and diving into the baptismal trying to wash off the smell of brimstone...

I’ve been involved in the explicitly neopagan end of the Druid scene for nearly a decade and a half now, and have cultivated a very close relationship with a few deities. Although lately the broader Neopagan world has become something that no longer resonates with me and I’ve been feeling strongly pulled towards the more Christian influenced ends of the Western Mystery tradition, and I have begun discovering perspectives on Jesus through authors like Dion Fortune, Gareth Knight, and others that are wholly unrecognizable compared to what I grew up with with and in my meditations, rituals, and prayers I’ve genuinely begun to feel an opening up towards a different kind of perspective on Jesus that I could gladly accept and allow into my life as a part of my spiritual existence. You’ve previously made two different suggestions regarding reconciling with Jesus for people in this situation: to one, you said that it was sufficient to keep walking the path they were walking and open a space for Jesus alongside their other gods. To another you said that trying to reconcile a non-Christian spirituality with existing Karmic ties to Jesus from baptism was lukewarm spirituality and that they’d need to drop the non-Christian aspects of their spirituality and build a foundation of devotional prayer to the Christian God and contemplative bible reading that should form the base of all the spiritual work they did. These seem like wildly different pieces of advice, one considerably more restrictive than the other. (In the latter, you invoked Revelation 3:16, which naturally brings to mind the various lurid definitions of what it meant to be spewed out that I grew up with).

Despite no longer being a neopagan, I do still have strong ties to my immediate community (which so far has avoided the toxicity that goes on outside) and even stronger ties to my gods that I have no intention of giving up and I’ve personally never gotten any inner stirrings that I’d have to (in fact, the primary deity I have a relationship with, Manannan seems to get along supremely well with Jesus and other Christian powers as do several other Irish deities). But I did want to get a better perspective on your differing answers there, and why in one case cultivating a relationship with Jesus while continuing to practice a non-Christian spirituality is acceptable while in another case it’s waffling.

replies.title: Re: Baptism and Karma Continued
replies.content:
1) I rather like your typo -- "the Christian Cod"! Nah, we're not talking eternal tarnation or anything of the kind. It's simply that once you accept baptism, you've got a connection with that deity, and it's not going to go away. If you don't feel comfortable relating to the Christian god in this lifetime, you'll do so in another life.

2) I think you've misremembered the situation about which I cited Revelations 3:16. Wasn't that the guy who wanted to be a devout Christian and still dabble in various other things? I know quite a few polytheists who have a relationship with Christ among other deities; they get good results, because they're approaching him as polytheists offering reverence to one god among many. That's different from claiming to be a Christian, and then not following through on what that religion teaches.

replies.title: Re: Baptism and Karma Continued
replies.content:
Well it is the dispensation of the Piscean Age so Cod fits!

I remember the conversation with the traditional Catholic who wanted to practice magic where you brought up that scripture as well. No, this was the more recent conversation with the person who kept going back and forth between an intuitive knowledge of a need to reconcile with Jesus and trying to find a non-Christian religious identity, and was considering some form of Christian occultism. Your recommendation was to either accept Christianity or reject it, and if they chose accept, to pick up daily contemplative bible reading and devotional prayer for several months before doing anything.

On a second reading it comes off as a bit more of a “pick a path and get on with it” nudge at someone who hasn’t set out on a path yet than an admonition for everyone who had ever been a Christian in their adult life to drop everything and pick up a Bible.

Thanks for clearing up what you meant by that, I’m certainly willing to continue to build on a relationship with Jesus, and I can even read the Bible and find potent seed thoughts for meditation and powerful truths instead of getting into shouting matches with it these days, but I’m not sure I can quite bring myself to go back to the “three chapters of the Bible for every one chapter of anything I read that’s not the Bible” rule.

replies.title: Re: Baptism and Karma Continued
replies.content:
I certainly don't recommend the three-to-one rule! The daily chapter of Bible reading is something I recommend to Christians because, as I noted, it was taught to me by one of the most spiritual people I've ever met, and she was a Christian. She used to insist that more than a chapter a day was way too much, since you wouldn't absorb it all -- and we talked about the three-to-one rule, too, and she said she'd be in deep trouble in that case, since she was a passionate reader and couldn't possibly limit herself to a third of a chapter a day. ;-)

But for those of us who don't feel a strong personal need to reconcile with Christianity, and simply want to acknowledge Christ as a god worthy of personal reverence, the range of options broadens out considerably.

comments.title: CGDish Stuff
comments.content:
A couple of Celtic Golden Dawn-adjacent questions.

1. Wanting to expand my devotional practices, I re-read your Druidry Handbook, specifically looking at the Grove Ceremonies and the Wheel of the Year. I noticed that the goddess Ana as earth-mother is not present in the CGD system, and I was wondering why. Or, in a Tree of Life context, could she be seen as goddess of the Unmanifest, as the soil from which the Tree of Life grows?

2. So I did a bit of geomancy on the question of the Wheel of the Year. The answer to "What would be the result if I were to celebrate the Wheel of the Year using the ceremonies laid out in the Druidry Handbook exactly as given?" was RW: Coch, LW: Mab, J: Carchar, with Tristwch as Reconciler. I took this as "Don't do this. Don't. Just don't." I then asked, "Will I one day be able to work out a modified Wheel of the Year practice, working with just those deities included in the CGD work?" Here I got RW: Bendith Fach, LW: Ffordd, J: Bendith Fawr. So, this is likely to be more successful, although the Reconciler was Gwyn, which suggested to me that it might be an intellectual exercise as much as anything else. Do you have any thoughts?

3. I've been using the tool of discursive meditation to work through the Tree of Life from top to bottom, also making use of material you've shared privately (you know the one). The results were very fruitful-- at least at first. But then I got down to the lower spheres on the Tree, and things started to get difficult. Specifically, I found that, upon making contact with the 5th path, between Byw and Ner, emotional traumas that I've dealt with over the last year and a half started surging up to the forefront of my mind. It was particularly bad the day I meditated on that path, and in fact came up right after.

For a few days it persisted. I'd meditate in the morning, then throughout the day painful memories, along with very painful emotions, would push their way to the front of my mind. The year that went from Beltane 2018 to Beltane 2019 was extremely painful for me, including losing a long-time relationship, losing my unborn baby, and even losing my cat, and all of this came up-- but so did much older pain, rooted in childhood sexual trauma.

I decided to stop practicing the meditations, and figure out what was going on. I wondered if I hadn't quite literally broken into the "Treasure House of Images" at Ner and let loose a lot of stuff that I've been repressing. The 5th path in particular, connecting as it does the spheres that govern the subtle body and the emotions, may relate to the ways that emotional traumas become *stuck* in the energetic system of the subtle body.

Last night I did some divinations around whether or not I should continue with this particular series of meditations. I do not like the idea of quitting, and in my life in general I'm trying to cultivate the energy of completing what I start. I first asked what the result would be if I started up again today, which would have meant meditating on the 7th path from Ner back up to Muner. I got RW: Pen y Ddraig, LW: Llosgwrn y Ddraig, J: Carchar. To me this wasn't necessarily a bad sign, as Carchar can mean the kind of stability that comes with limits and completed work, and Llosgwrn Y Ddraig governs the 7th path. Still, those aren't the best figures. So I asked, "Is it the view of Hesus and of the powers that guide the DOGD that it would be a good idea for me to start meditating on the Tree of Life again tomorrow?" Now I got RW: Bendith Fawr, LW: Pobl, J: Bendith Fawr. I took this as a "Yep," and so I meditated on the 7th path this morning.

Sorry for the long preamble. My questions, then, are:

3.a. In your view, is my interpretation of what was happening correct? Did I release repressed trauma by digging into the manifestations of the spheres and paths in question in my own microcosm?

3.b. Is this kind of thing normal?

3.c. Was it a good idea to return to meditating on the Tree, after 3 days absence? Or should I have given it more time? Or should I have given it up entirely?

Ritual

Oof. Is that the latest bit of kindergarten ritual from the soi-disant Magic Resistance? I see they haven't learned much. Your points are good, and I'd add a few comments:

1) Let's imagine the spirits of those Republicans show up. On the one hand, there's the image of Trump, who is a successful GOP politician in what used to be the mainstream of the party. On the other, you've got a member of the Magic Resistance, with the beliefs, values, and ideologies common to that end of the political spectrum, and who are, as you point out, spewing rage at the GOP. Exactly who are the spirits going to find the most suitable target for their outrage?

2) Yes, and it's worse than that. Asking people to see the truth is lethal if you're lying. How many wars has Trump started, for example? As written, the ritual would most likely help voters see through the barrage of media propaganda and make them more likely to support Trump -- and of course it could be gimmicked by opponents to make that the natural outcome.

3) True, and putting that into the ritual will help Trump finish the process of chasing out the Never Trumpers and RINOs and remaking the GOP in his populist image. I'm sure this is what the Resistance wanted... ;-)

4) I'd also point out the following:

5) The color red is used cross-culturally in magic as an emblem of power and vitality. Bracketing Trump's photo between two pieces of red cloth would be understood in magical traditions from China to Dahomey as a way of blessing Trump.

6) "May Trump see justice" is wonderfully ambiguous. It could be satisfied just as well by a sudden epiphany in which Trump becomes even more committed to the pursuit of justice. It would be child's play, too, for the other side to reword that lightly so that Trump sees a whole series of justices, all of them appointed by him, moving into the federal courts...

comments.title: Accidental Mysticism
comments.content:
Hello, JMG and forum. This is Evi; she of the spiritual crisis a couple of weeks ago. I’d like to thank everyone for their encouragement and advice. It’s definitely helped me approach the issue from a different and hopefully more fruitful angle. Concerning which:

I’ve been a lurker here for quite a while; the wide range of topics and viewpoints is fascinating. Not infrequently, mysticism receives mention. One such mention last week got me thinking.

I’ve sometimes referred to myself, half-jokingly, as an “accidental mystic”. I’ve had a couple of what I can only call spontaneous mystical experiences over my lifetime - the classic ineffable experience of universal unity and enveloping love, so far as I can tell. I didn’t invite the experiences; actually, I fancied myself a teenage atheist the first time it happened. No drugs were involved.

I spent the next decade-plus half-heartedly researching religions, looking for one that actually preached a deity who matched my experience, but none quite did. I have nothing against polytheism; in fact, I don’t doubt in the slightest that the universe is host to beings of all types and powers. But I also can’t help “knowing” that there is Something Else beneath and beyond and throughout all of being-ness, and *that* is the force that I yearn to deepen my relationship with.

During a time of great despair and worsening physical disability (a chronic pain/fatigue condition that is resistant to treatment), I had a second experience, very like the first. Within a few months, I found myself in the back pew of the local Episcopal church, desperate for answers, for a way to connect more effectively with the “Godsense” that was now a vague constant in the back of my mind and heart. But the Christian God, at least as usually described to me, wasn’t quite the God that I “knew”, either, and the church’s rites and practices did little to deepen my sense of God. When I moved away from that church, I gave up on the Christian religion for several years.

I have read some about mysticism, but most of the contemporary stuff seems to be new age word salad aping the style of the old stuff, which is mostly impenetrable advice on intensive methods to court mystical experiences. I don’t blame it for being impenetrable; that can’t be helped given the topic. But it’s not what I’m looking for.

I’m not necessarily *pursuing* more mystical experiences; I’ve had them already, and I’m still processing the repercussions. What I want to know is, how do you live your life once you have that knowing? How do you open yourself to the God in the back of your heart, and actually listen to it moment by moment and live in attunement with it? How do you deepen and honor the relationship? I can’t quite seem to figure it out, and it’s beyond frustrating.

I’m working on bible study and prayer, as you recommended. Reading “The Pagan Christ” is helping a lot with approaching the scriptures usefully, as inspired mythology. I’m also grateful to the individual who reminded me of Rohr; I admire what I’ve read from him previously.

Prayer is… hard. My mental health has been poor enough since I became disabled that I don’t necessarily trust any “answers” I get, which is why I want to deepen my sense of the divine. It’s a bit of a chicken-and-egg problem.

I’m intrigued by operative magic, but not necessarily drawn to perform it. I doubt my capacity to physically sustain an intensive daily practice of such, at any rate. I’m also intrigued by the Cabala, especially given how strongly I’m drawn to the Tree of Life as a symbol for God. But most of what I’ve found on that has been impenetrable in it’s own way, and the readable stuff is geared to practical magic and I’m not quite sure what to do with it.

I have the strong suspicion, based on dreams, half-waking “visions”, deja vu, etc., that I’ve experienced at least two previous incarnations as a monastic - a Japanese Zen monk, and a European Catholic nun. Unfortunately, there aren’t enough details to provide me with much insight.

Sorry for the long post. As you can see, I don't know what to call myself, why I am the way I am, whether those details matter at all, or what to do with myself if they don't. Obviously, if I'm any kind of mystic, I'm not a very good one. I’m not even quite sure what I’m asking for here - advice, insight, encouragement, commiseration? All are welcome. (And I *am* bearing in mind the advice to focus on lectio divina for a couple of months. I just can't help thinking ahead, or about all of my other uncertainties...)

I'm glad that you're finding these discussions helpful! As for what to call yourself, there's a definite panache in "accidental mystic' -- why not use that for the moment, and see what calls you?

comments.title: Divination and intuition
comments.content:
I am pondering a question regarding the relation between divination and intuition for a while now. Divination can provide you a kind of perception you usually don't have. However, human beings seem to have a similar perception, in its capacity ranging from nearly not existent to something we could possibly call clairvoyance. There are for example questions I can ask an oracle of which I almost know the answer beforehand and the oracle (more or less) confirms the obvious, while it's fair to say that what "obvious" means is highly subjective. For some observer, the insight might be "magic", for some other it might be just, well, obvious. But there are also questions, that I can ask and I have no clue and no feeling on what's right or wrong - there I am completely dependent on the oracle. Above that, there will be a whole lot of questions I can't even ask, because they are too far beyond my capacity to perceive and understand.

How to approach divination in a way, that it improves your natural perception? If, for example, somebody put a very strong focus on divination in this life, how would that effect the person in this and the successive incarnations? On the contrary, what are in your experience flawed approaches that might cripple your natural faculties?

The more I think about it, the more it seems to me, that ethics and self-control are crucial factors when going into that direction. Thought to the very extreme end, who would want to be a human oracle? I assume it might need a huge amount of detachment, self-control and also sacrifice to serve as one without breaking...

Thank's for your thoughts on this!
Nachtgurke

replies.title: Re: Divination and intuition
replies.content: The whole point of practicing divination using a formal oracle -- for example, geomancy, or runes, or Tarot -- is to get to the point at which you don't need to practice divination any more, because your intuition is running 24/7. Intuition, according to occult philosophy, is one of the functions of the mental body, and as each of us develops the capacity to perceive and act on the mental plane, and the vague and unformed "mental sheath" each of us has develops into the first rough sketch of a mental body, the intuitive powers become normal. That's why the student of occultism practices daily divination.

comments.title: Hecate
comments.content:
Dear JMG,
During a meditation, I met the Nun I see in my mind's eye during morning prayers. I asked if she was the Lady Hecate and she said she was. I asked why she was appearing to me as a Nun and she said she answers prayers sent to a number of Nuns and has become accustomed to appearing as a Nun. I asked why a Nun and she said it fits her being at once a maiden (a virgin), a mother (Mother Superior) and a crone (elder female). Granted this might all be a product of my imagination but it makes sense to me that the Gods will receive prayers even if people use a different name.

In fact, I know of one case where a girl in Ireland prayed to a Saint for help and her prayers were answered by an old shepherd some years deceased. He had a friend in the area who was compelled to go to the girl's house and the girl led him to the barn and he was able to deliver the milk cow of her calf.

Without his help, the family would have been in very bad circumstances and he saved both cow and calf. The girl gave him a cup of tea and described the vision she had of the Saint which was a very accurate description of the old shepherd. So it seems we may direct our prayers to certain beings but they may not be the ones who deal with the prayer.

So that is why the Lady Hecate may be worshiped as a Catholic saint.

Maxine Rogers

replies.title: Re: Hecate
replies.content:
An anecdote: In the Orthodox tradition, the saint you ask for help in finding lost things is typically St. Phanourios. He's extremely popular, and if he finds your lost item, you bake him a cake (phanouropita) and share it with everybody at coffee hour.

There's a hitch: St. Phanourios probably never existed. He's not in the official synaxarion, because there's basically zero evidence he was real. The origin story there strongly suggests that someone misread a damaged icon of St. George ("the revealer"), to invent him. He doesn't seem bothered by his non-existence: he continues to find our car keys, wallets, lost baptismal crosses, library books, etc. It seems obvious there is someone on the other end of the line.

One does wonder if perhaps some of the people we venerate have multiple storefronts. Does St. George answer us when we ask the "holy revealer" for help? Does the call go to some cosmic help desk where the clerk says: "oh, another lost wedding ring, let's put that one through to St. Anthony..."? No idea, but I still baked the cake after my car key turned up.

Mark Twain retails a couple of fine old country remedies for warts in chapter 6 of "Tom Sawyer," including an elegant one using "spunk-water" (water taked from a hollow in an old rotten stump out in the woods) and a less pleasant one that uses a dried-up, long dead dead cat.

Wart-cures are very, very numerous, and generally are said by their users to have a good rate of success, whatever the method used. A academic colleague told me once about his father's wart cure, which (IIRC) consisted of putting some blood from your wart onn a coin, tossing the coin backwards over your shoulder into a pasture, and leaving the place without even the quickest glance backward to see where the coin might have landed.

replies.title: Re: Warts
replies.content:
Around here, my grandmother's generation's wart cure went like this:

Take an apple. Cut it in half (or cut a slice out of it). Rub the cut surface on the wart, then put the apple back together and bury it by moonlight. Some people seemed to think the phase of the moon was important here, but I do not know what phase is the right one, alas. Folks who'd had it done swore that it worked, and also that some people could "witch" warts away better than others. The idea was that as the apple rotted away, so would the wart.

replies.title: Re: Warts
replies.content: I would expect that the waning moon would be the right period for doing this cure. The maxim among witches is that the first crescent is the best time to begin something, the entire period of the moon's increase is good for growth and bringing things to you. When you want to get rid of something, do it when the moon's light is decreasing.

replies.title: Re: Warts
replies.content: I had a doctor tell me she often "bought" them for a quarter off her younger patients. That usually caused them to go away, she said.

replies.title: Re: First geomancy reading
replies.content:
Okay, when Rubeus appears as the first mother of a reading you're doing for yourself, it often implies that you're not being entirely honest with yourself about the subject of the reading. Other than that, it's a favorable reading, but you might want to reflect on whether you're fooling yourself in some way.

Coins are fine, btw; my favorite 41 cent geomancy set consists of a quarter, a dime, a nickel, and a penny. Toss 'em in the air and see how they land: the quarter is the top line, the dime the second, and so on.

comments.title: Planets and currents
comments.content:

replies.title: Re: Planets and currents
replies.content:
1) No, the three currents are more general. All astrological influences are modifications of the solar current. All geosophic influences (spiritual influences of places) are modifications of the telluric current. All modes of individual consciousness are modifications of the lunar current.

2) Exactly! On Mars, Earth would be an important astrological planet.

comments.title: realism vs. cynicism or callous dismissal
comments.content:
This is not exactly a question about magic but--- I can recall being taught that the doctrine of reincarnation caused Asians to be fatalistic and callous about the fate of lower caste or lower class members of their societies. Poor, sick, crippled? Oh well, your bad karma, good luck with that. Obviously some Christians also managed to convince themselves that "rich man in his castle, poor man in his hall" was God's will and not to be rebelled against by those on the bottom. Now add to this the feeling that humans are too numerous and inflicting too much damage on the rest of the world and the realism of knowing that the current system simply can't endure or expand to any great extent due to resource limits and all the other things you discussed on the Archdruid Report. Sometimes I feel like there is a danger of becoming callous or cynical about the inevitable suffering that will result. It seems too easy to brush it off with a conviction that the process is necessary for all the souls involved to learn and progress. Comments?

Thanks for all your work.

RER

replies.title: Re: realism vs. cynicism or callous dismissal
replies.content: There are an infinite number of excuses for callousness, and yes, those are among them. I like to point out -- and also to reflect on! -- the fact that none of us have any way of knowing the karma of any other person; it may be that the karma of the person you next see is to be helped by a passing stranger, i.e., you. Equally, whatever their fate may be, your fate depends on how you treat others, right here, right now...

comments.title: divination
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

A couple of months ago I had a decision to make where I had identified three options. I did a divination for each option asking "What will be the outcome if do X"?

The results seemed very clear: options A and B would lead to bad outcomes and option C to good. I decided on option C (although not solely because of the divination. I don't trust my powers that much yet having only about a year of practice with tarot).

Almost immediately after there were negative consequences as a result of taking option C. Nothing too drastic. Some small financial losses and general inconvenience. I came to realise that the issues were at least partly of my own making. I had not set things up properly for option C to work.

In that aftermath of all that, it has become clear to me that option A is the best option. It is the one that I actually want to take and I believe that I have prepared for so it should work. I have done another divination which backs up my intuition on this.

I'm now a little confused around my original divination. Did I interpret the cards correctly or incorrectly? Option C seems to have worked out in the longer run, but in the short run the results were not how I interpreted the cards. Is it also possible that my original question was too open ended to give meaningful answers? After all, larger and more important decisions don't have a single outcome but a cascading series of outcomes some of which can be good or bad.

Thanks as always for your response,
Simon

replies.title: Re: divination
replies.content: Divination's tricky. You may have misinterpreted the reading. Alternatively, it may be that you had to go through option C to be able to make A work the way it should. This is one of the reasons I don't encourage anyone to make important decisions on the basis of a single divination!
